Natural Numbers and Whole Numbers: Properties of operations on Whole Numbers
topics.description: Introduction to counting numbers (Natural) and the inclusion of zero (Whole numbers), along with the properties of the four fundamental arithmetic operations (Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication, Division).
Concepts of factors and multiples, leading to the classification of numbers into Even, Odd, Prime, Composite, Twin Primes, and Co-Prime Numbers, including the method of prime factorization.
Methods to determine the Highest Common Factor (HCF) and Least Common Multiple (LCM) of numbers using prime factorization and division methods, and understanding the relationship between them.
A set of rules to quickly check if a number is divisible by 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9, or 11 without performing the actual division.

chapters.title: INTEGERS
topics: Creating a new number for every natural number
Representation of Integers on Number line
Addition and Subtraction of integers using Number line

topics.description: This section covers the rules and properties for performing addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division with integers.

chapters.title: SQUARE, SQUAREROOT AND CUBE, CUBE ROOT

topics.description: Introduction to the concept of squares, including the squares of natural numbers and the property that the square of a negative number is a positive number. Also discusses properties like the square of an even number is even and an odd number is odd.

Square root: Finding a square root of a perfect square by factorisation method
Finding a square root of a perfect square by division method
Some problems based on Square root
topics.description: Defining the square root of a number and learning methods to find it, such as prime factorisation and the long division method. Includes solving word problems using the concept of square roots.

Cube and Cube root: Making any number a Perfect Cubic Number
topics.description: Defining the cube of a number, identifying perfect cube numbers, and understanding the process of finding the smallest number to multiply or divide to make a given number a perfect cube.

topics: Finding a cube root of a perfect cube by Prime Factorisation method

topics.description: This section explains how to apply basic arithmetic operations such as addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division to algebraic expressions involving variables and constants.

This topic introduces the concept of like and unlike terms, and details the procedures for performing addition, subtraction, and multiplication on these terms, including the multiplication of variables with exponents.

chapters.title: ALGEBRAIC EXPRESSIONS AND OPERATIONS

topics.description: Introduction to algebraic expressions as combinations of constants and variables, and the identification of their constituent terms and coefficients.

topics: 5.2 Co-efficient of the terms with two or more variables
5.3 Like and Unlike terms of Algebraic Expressions

topics.description: Classification of algebraic expressions based on the number of terms.
Techniques for combining algebraic expressions through addition and subtraction.

chapters.title: LINEAR EQUATIONS IN ONE VARIABLE
topics: To convert word problems to Algebraic Equations.
ax + b = 0 is a linear equation in one variable.

topics.description: Introduces the concept of an equation by converting statements into mathematical forms.

6.2 Linear Equations: Definition: Linear Equation in one variable is such an equation which has one variable and degree 1.
topics.description: Defines linear equations in one variable, focusing on the degree of the equation and providing examples from real-life situations.

6.3 Solving Linear Equations: Definition: That value of the variable which makes the equation a 'True Statement' is called the solution of the equation.
By adding an expression on both sides
By subtracting an expression from both sides
By dividing or multiplying by some non-zero number to both sides
topics.description: Explains the process of finding the solution to a linear equation by performing the same operations on both sides to maintain equality.

topics: Solving daily life problems with simple language through linear equations.

Ratio: Terms of Ratio: Antecedent and Consequent
Ratio as a fraction in its lowest term
topics.description: Introduces the concept of ratio as a relation between two similar quantities, its representation using the ':' symbol or as a fraction, and the terms used to define it.

Proportion: Terms of Proportion: Extremes and Means
topics.description: Defines proportion as the equality of two ratios and explains the relationship where the product of the extremes equals the product of the means.
Explains the two main types of proportions: direct, where quantities change in the same way, and inverse, where an increase in one quantity causes a decrease in the other.

Covers practical applications of ratio and proportion, including the unitary method for finding the value of a single unit, solving problems related to work done over time, and calculating speed, distance, and time, including relative speed.
Understanding that a fraction with a denominator of 100 is called a percent. This section covers converting fractions to percentages and percentages back to fractions.

Find out a specific percent of a given a: Some word problems based on percentage
topics.description: Methods for calculating a specific percentage of a given quantity and solving word problems related to percentage calculations.
Introduces the concepts of Cost Price (CP) and Selling Price (SP). Defines profit as SP - CP and loss as CP - SP, and explains how to calculate profit or loss as a percentage of the cost price.
Explains discount as a reduction on the 'market price' of an article. The amount paid by the customer is the Selling Price (SP).
An introduction to the fundamental concepts of borrowing money, where the additional amount paid back is called Interest. It establishes the relationship: Amount = Principal + Interest.

9.1 Simple Interest: Formula for Simple Interest: I = Prt / 100
topics.description: Covers the calculation of interest on the initial principal for the entire loan period.

9.2 Compound Interest: Formula for Compound Amount: A = P(1+r)^n
Formula for Compound Interest: C = A - P
Calculating Principal (P) when A, r, and n are given
Calculating Rate (r) when A, P, and n are given
Calculating Time (n) when A, P, and r are given
topics.description: Explains interest calculation where the interest for each period is added to the principal for calculating the next period's interest.
Introduces the fundamental, undefined concepts of geometry: point (a location), line (a straight path extending infinitely), and plane (a flat surface extending infinitely).
Defines a line segment as a part of a line with two endpoints and a fixed length, and a ray as a part of a line with one starting point extending infinitely in one direction.

10.3 Lines in a Plane: A line passing through two points
topics.description: Explores the properties of lines on a plane, establishing that only one line can pass through two distinct points, and classifying pairs of lines as intersecting, parallel, or coincident.
Defines collinear points as three or more points lying on the same line, and concurrent lines as three or more lines that intersect at a single common point.

10.5 Open and Closed Shapes: 10.5.2 Inner and outer parts of simple closed figures
topics.description: Distinguishes between open and closed figures (curves) and introduces the concept of simple figures. It explains how a simple closed figure divides a plane into three parts: the interior, the exterior, and the figure itself.
Describes the standard instruments in a geometry box—ruler, dividers, compass, set-squares, and protractor—and explains their specific functions in drawing and measuring.

10.7-10.9 Measurement and Drawing of a L: Using the scale to draw a line segment of given measure
topics.description: Details the process of measuring the length of a line segment using a ruler and provides step-by-step instructions for drawing a line segment of a specific length.
Step-by-step instructions for constructing specific angles using a ruler and compass.
Classification of different types of angles based on their degree measure.
Defining and identifying relationships between pairs of angles.
Introduction to parallel lines, transversals, and the properties of angles formed, including corresponding, alternate, and interior angles.

Drawing Parallel and Perpendicular Lines: Drawing Parallel Lines with a set-square
Drawing Parallel Lines with a ruler and compass
Drawing a line perpendicular to a given line
topics.description: Methods for constructing parallel and perpendicular lines using various geometrical tools.

chapters.title: TRIANGLES AND ITS TYPES
topics: 12.3 Vertices, Sides, Angles, Exterior Angles, Vertically opposite Angles of Triangle
12.5 Sum of Angles of a Triangle
12.6 Relation between the exterior angle and interior opposite angles
12.7 Sum of any two sides of a Triangle

12.8 Categorisation of Triangles: On the bases of sides (Scalene, Isosceles, Equilateral)
On the bases of angles (Acute, Right, Obtuse)
topics.description: Classification of triangles into different types based on the lengths of their sides and the measures of their angles.

topics: 12.10 Property of Sides and Angles of Isosceles Triangles

chapters.title: QUADRILATERALS AND ITS TYPES

topics.description: Discusses the basic components of a quadrilateral, including its four sides, four corners (vertices), four angles, and two diagonals, along with the concepts of adjacent and opposite sides/angles.

topics: Sum of angles of a Quadrilateral

topics.description: Introduces and defines various special types of quadrilaterals based on their properties regarding sides and angles.

chapters.title: CIRCLE
topics: Introduction to circles through real-life examples like grazing animals and various circular objects.

topics.description: This section defines the fundamental components of a circle, including its center, radius, diameter, circumference, chord, arc, segment, and sector.

Drawing a Circle: Drawing a circle of given radius
topics.description: Explains the method to draw a circle of a specific radius using a pair of compasses and a ruler.

topics: Measure of an angle in a semi-circle
Distance of chords from the centre

topics.description: Introduction to congruent figures, which have the same shape and size, using examples from daily life. The method of superposition is explained to verify congruency. The topic then focuses on the congruency of triangles and the concept of corresponding sides and angles.
Details the four primary rules for verifying the congruency of two triangles without needing to check all six corresponding elements. Each rule is explained with illustrative figures.

Symmetry: 15.5 Number of Axis or Lines of symmetry
topics.description: Defines symmetric figures as those that can be folded along a line (axis of symmetry) so that one part completely covers the other. It distinguishes between symmetrical and asymmetrical shapes and explains that different figures can have different numbers of axes of symmetry.

Introduction to Area and Perimeter: 16.3 Standard units of measure of area
topics.description: Introduces the fundamental concepts of area as the region covered by a plane figure and perimeter as the length of its boundary. It also covers the standard units for measuring area.

Area and Perimeter of Triangles: 16.5 Area of a triangle with the help of Graph Paper
Area of triangle using Heron's formulas
topics.description: Details methods for calculating the area of a triangle, including estimation using graph paper, the standard formula (1/2 * base * height), and Heron's formula when side lengths are known.

Perimeter and Area of Rectangles and Squ: 16.9 Area of a rectangle and square
topics.description: Covers the formulas and applications for calculating the perimeter and area of rectangles and squares.

Area of Parallelograms and Trapeziums: 16.10 Area of a parallelogram from by graph paper
topics.description: Explains how to find the area of a parallelogram using both graphical methods and the base-height formula, and introduces the formula for the area of a trapezium.

Circumference and Area of a Circle: Relation between circumference and the diameter of circle
topics.description: Defines the circumference and area of a circle, explains the relationship with the diameter via the constant π, and provides the formulas for calculation.
An introduction to cuboidal shapes found in daily life, covering the concepts of faces, edges, vertices, surface area, and volume.

Faces, Edges and Vertices of a Cuboid: 17.2 Edges and Vertices of a Cuboid
topics.description: Discusses the six rectangular faces, twelve edges, and eight vertices of a cuboid.
Defining a cube as a special type of cuboid where all edges are of equal length, resulting in square faces.

Surface area of a cube and cuboid: Formula for surface area of a cuboid: 2(lb + bh + hl)
Formula for surface area of a cube: 6 × (side)²
topics.description: Explains that the surface area is the sum of the areas of all six faces and provides the respective formulas for cuboids and cubes.

Volume of a cube and cuboid: 17.6 The volume of a cube and cuboid
topics.description: Defines volume as the measure of three-dimensional space occupied by a solid and provides formulas for calculating the volume of a cuboid (l × b × h) and a cube (side³).
Introduction to the concept of data and the two main types of data collection: primary data collected directly from the source, and secondary data collected from existing records.

Presentation of Data: Use of frequency to present data
Classification of data in different categories
topics.description: Methods for organizing and presenting raw data, including sorting, creating frequency distribution tables with tally marks, calculating the range, and grouping data into relevant categories.

Bar Graph: How to draw bar graph on a graph sheet
topics.description: A visual method of data representation using bars. Covers interpretation, construction, and the importance of selecting an appropriate scale for clarity.
A circular statistical graphic used to represent numerical proportions. The chapter explains how to read and construct pie charts by calculating the central angle for each data segment.

chapters.title: INTRODUCTION OF VEDIC MATHEMATICS

topics.description: An introduction to the ancient Indian system of mathematics, highlighting its benefits for learners, such as developing interest, increasing confidence for competitive exams, saving time in calculations, and improving reasoning and memory power.

topics: 19.2 Vedic Mathematics Sutras and their meaning

topics.description: Introduces the concept of Vinculum, a method of using positive and negative digits together to simplify number operations by converting large numbers into smaller ones.

Presents different methods for addition in Vedic Mathematics, including the left-to-right approach, the Shunyant sutra (making a number end with zero), and the Nikhilam sutra for adding numbers around a base.

Multiplication: Multiplication - first method - Sutra एकन्यूनेन पूर्वेण
Multiplication Sutra - (Sum of ones digits is 10)
topics.description: Learning various sutras for multiplying numbers, including special cases like multiplying by 9s, numbers near a base or sub-base, and a universal method for any number.

Squaring: To find the square - sutra यावदूनं
topics.description: Methods to find the square of numbers, including those near a base and a general method for any two-digit number.
Techniques for finding the cube of numbers using the Yavadunam sutra for numbers near a base and the Anurupyena sutra.

Square Root and Cube Root: Cube root of six or less than six digit numbers
topics.description: Finding the square root and cube root of perfect squares and cubes by observation (Vilokanam).

chapters.title: NUMBER SYSTEMS
topics: Introduction to the history and need for number systems

Recall of Natural Numbers, Whole Numbers: Representing Integers on the Number Line
topics.description: A review of the properties and operations on natural numbers, whole numbers, and integers, including their representation on the number line.

Rational Numbers: Standard form of a Rational Number
topics.description: Introduction to rational numbers, defined as numbers that can be expressed in the form p/q, and their properties.

topics: Equivalent Forms of a Rational Number
Rational Numbers on the Number Line

Four Fundamental Operations on Rational: Multiplication and Division of Rational Numbers
topics.description: Performing addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division with rational numbers.

topics: Decimal Representation of a Rational Number
Expressing Decimal Expansion of a Rational Number in p/q Form
Rational Numbers Between Two Rational Numbers
Finding an Irrational Number Between Two Given Numbers
Rounding Off Numbers to a Given Number of Decimal Places

topics.description: Introduction to exponential notation to represent repeated multiplication, defining the base and the exponent.

Laws of Exponents: Laws of Exponents for Integral Exponents
topics.description: Covers the fundamental rules for operations involving exponents, including multiplication, division, and powers of powers for positive, zero, negative, and rational exponents.
Introduces surds as positive irrational numbers, defining their components like index and radicand, and classifying them as pure or mixed.
Explains how to perform arithmetic operations on surds by changing them to similar surds or surds of the same order.

This section defines algebraic expressions as combinations of numbers, variables, and operations. It introduces the concept of terms and coefficients, and then defines polynomials as a special type of algebraic expression with specific rules for exponents and variables. It also classifies polynomials based on the number of terms.

This section covers the four fundamental arithmetic operations as they apply to polynomials, including combining like terms for addition and subtraction, using the distributive property for multiplication, and long division for polynomials.

This section introduces frequently occurring algebraic products, also known as algebraic formulae, which save time in multiplication. It covers squares and cubes of binomials, the product of a sum and difference, and other key identities, including geometrical verifications.

Factorization of Polynomials: Factorization Involving the Difference of Two Squares
Factorization of a Perfect Square Trinomial
Factorization of a Polynomial Reducible to the Difference of Two Squares
Factorization of Polynomials Involving Sum or Difference of Two Cubes
Factorising Trinomials by Splitting the Middle Term
topics.description: This topic covers the process of writing a polynomial as a product of two or more polynomials (its factors). It details various methods based on special product identities and the technique of splitting the middle term for trinomials.
This section explains how to find the Highest Common Factor (HCF) and the Lowest Common Multiple (LCM) of two or more polynomials by first factorizing them.

Operations on Rational Expressions: Addition and Subtraction of Rational Expressions
Multiplication and Division of Rational Expressions
topics.description: Defines rational expressions as algebraic expressions in the form of P/Q where P and Q are polynomials. It covers the four fundamental operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division) on these expressions.

chapters.title: LINEAR EQUATIONS

OBJECTIVES: Identify and cite examples of linear equations.
Write and solve a linear equation in one variable.
Cite examples and write linear equations in two variables.
Draw the graph of a linear equation in two variables.
Find the solution of a linear equation in two variables.
Find the solution of a system of two linear equations graphically and algebraically.
Translate real life problems in terms of linear equations and solve them.
topics.description: An overview of the learning outcomes after studying this lesson on linear equations.
Defining linear equations in one and two variables, distinguishing them from non-linear equations, and identifying their general forms.

topics: 5.2 FORMATION OF LINEAR EQUATIONS IN ONE VARIABLE
5.3 SOLUTION OF LINEAR EQUATIONS IN ONE VARIABLE
5.6 GRAPH OF A LINEAR EQUATION IN TWO VARIABLES

topics.description: Exploring methods to solve a pair of linear equations simultaneously, including graphical and algebraic approaches.

Introduction to polynomials of degree two, known as quadratic polynomials, and their corresponding equations when equated to zero. It covers identifying quadratic equations from a collection of equations.
Defining the standard form of a quadratic equation as ax² + bx + c = 0, where a, b, and c are constants and a ≠ 0. This section includes examples of converting equations to the standard form.

6.3 SOLUTION OF A QUADRATIC EQUATION: Nature of roots using the Discriminant
topics.description: This section explains two algebraic methods for finding the roots or solutions of a quadratic equation. It covers solving by factorization and by using the quadratic formula. It also introduces the concept of the discriminant (D = b² - 4ac) to determine the nature of the roots (real and distinct, real and equal, or no real roots).
Application of quadratic equations to solve real-world problems by translating them into mathematical equations.

In your daily life you must have observed that in nature, many things follow patterns such as petals of flowers, the holes of a honey-comb, the spirals on a pine apple etc. In this lesson, you will study one special type of number pattern called Arithmetic Progression (AP). You will also learn to find general term and the sum of first n terms of an arithmetic progression.

OBJECTIVES: identify arithmetic progression from a given list of numbers;
determine the general term of an arithmetic progression;
find the sum of first n terms of an arithmetic progression.

7.2 ARITHMETIC PROGRESSION: Standard form of an AP: a, a + d, a + 2d, a + 3d, ...

7.4 SUM OF FIRST n TERMS OF AN AP: Formula for sum of first n terms: S_n = n/2 [2a + (n-1)d]
Formula for sum using the last term 'l': S_n = n/2 (a + l)

chapters.title: PERCENTAGE AND ITS APPLICATIONS
topics: Conversion of a Fraction into Percent and Vice Versa
Conversion of Decimal into a Percent and Vice Versa
Calculation of Percent of a Quantity or a Number

topics.description: This section discusses the application of percentage in different fields, including solving problems related to profit and loss, discount, simple interest, compound interest, rate of growth, and depreciation.

chapters.title: INSTALMENT BUYING

topics.description: Defines the key terms associated with purchasing goods on an instalment basis, where a customer pays a portion of the price upfront and the rest in periodic payments.

topics: To Find the Interest in an Instalment Plan
To Find the Amount of Instalment

Point, Line and Angle: Angle and its Types (Acute, Obtuse, Right, Straight)
topics.description: Introduces the basic geometric concepts of point, line, and plane, and the different types of angles.
Discusses various relationships between pairs of angles, such as adjacent, complementary, supplementary, linear pairs, and vertically opposite angles.

Lines and Transversal: Interior Angles on the Same Side of the Transversal
Properties of Angles when a Transversal intersects Parallel Lines
topics.description: Explores the angles formed when a transversal line intersects two or more lines, with a focus on the properties that arise when the lines are parallel.

Triangle, its Types and Properties: Types of Triangles (based on sides and angles)
Angle Sum Property of a Triangle
topics.description: Covers the classification of triangles and fundamental properties related to their interior and exterior angles.

Locus: Locus of a point equidistant from two given points
Locus of a point equidistant from two intersecting lines
topics.description: Introduces the concept of a locus as a path traced by a point moving under specific geometric conditions.

CONCEPT OF CONGRUENCE: Criteria for Congruence of Some Figures
topics.description: Introduces the concept of congruent figures as those having the same shape and size, with examples like line segments of equal length, squares with equal sides, and circles with equal radii.
Defines the condition for two triangles to be congruent, which is when all the sides and all the angles of one are equal to the corresponding sides and angles of the other.

CRITERIA FOR CONGRUENCE OF TRIANGLES: Criterion 2: ASA or AAS (Angle Side Angle or Angle Angle Side)
topics.description: Details the four criteria that can be used to prove the congruence of two triangles by knowing the equality of only three corresponding parts.

ANGLES OPPOSITE TO EQUAL SIDES OF A TRIA: Theorem: The angles opposite to equal sides of a triangle are equal.
Theorem: The sides opposite to equal angles of a triangle are equal.
topics.description: Proves the relationship between equal sides and their opposite angles in a triangle and its converse.

INEQUALITIES IN A TRIANGLE: Theorem: If two sides of a triangle are unequal, then the longer side has the greater angle opposite to it.
Theorem: In a triangle, the greater angle has the longer side opposite to it.
Theorem: The sum of any two sides of a triangle is greater than the third side.
topics.description: Explores the relationships among sides and angles of a triangle when they are unequal.

CONCURRENT LINES: Perpendicular Bisectors of the Sides of a Triangle
topics.description: This lesson studies the concurrency property of special lines in a triangle, such as medians, right bisectors of sides, angle bisectors, and altitudes. It defines these terms and verifies their concurrency, introducing the points of concurrency: incentre, circumcentre, orthocentre, and centroid.
Introduction to the definition of a quadrilateral, its vertices, opposite and adjacent sides, and opposite and adjacent angles.
Describes various types of quadrilaterals and their defining characteristics, presented with a family tree diagram.
Details the properties of sides, angles, and diagonals for different types of quadrilaterals, including converse properties.
Verifies the theorem that the line segment joining the mid-points of any two sides of a triangle is parallel to the third side and half of it, along with its converse.

Explains the theorem stating that if there are three or more parallel lines and the intercepts made by them on one transversal are equal, the corresponding intercepts on any other transversal are also equal.

Theorems on Area: 13.6 THE DIAGONAL OF A PARALLELOGRAM AND RELATION TO THE AREA
13.7 PARALLELOGRAMS AND TRIANGLES BETWEEN THE SAME PARALLELS
13.8 TRIANGLES ON THE SAME OR EQUAL BASES HAVING EQUAL AREAS HAVE THEIR CORRESPONDING ALTITUDES EQUAL
topics.description: Covers theorems related to the area of parallelograms and triangles, such as how a diagonal divides a parallelogram, properties of figures on the same base and between the same parallels, and the relationship between area, base, and altitude.

chapters.title: SIMILARITY OF TRIANGLES
topics: 14.3 BISECTOR OF AN ANGLE OF A TRIANGLE

topics.description: Defines the conditions for triangle similarity and introduces the three main criteria: Angle-Angle-Angle (AAA), Side-Side-Side (SSS), and Side-Angle-Side (SAS).

14.5 Further Results on Similar Triangle: Similarity in Right Triangles (Perpendicular to Hypotenuse)
Ratio of Areas of Similar Triangles
topics.description: Discusses two key results: the properties of triangles formed by an altitude to the hypotenuse of a right triangle, and the relationship between the areas of similar triangles and the squares of their corresponding sides.
Provides a proof of the Pythagoras Theorem using the concept of triangle similarity and states its converse, which is used to identify right triangles.

Introduces the definition of a circle and its fundamental components, including radius, chord, diameter, arc, semicircle, sector, segment, and circumference. It also distinguishes between the interior and exterior of a circle.
Explains how to measure the length of an arc and defines minor and major arcs based on their length relative to a semicircle.

SOME IMPORTANT RULES: Relationship between arcs, chords, and angles at the centre
Perpendicular from the centre to a chord
Equal chords and their distance from the centre
topics.description: Covers key theorems and properties of circles, such as the relationship between congruent arcs and the angles they subtend at the centre, the property that a perpendicular from the centre bisects a chord, the uniqueness of a circle through three points, and the property that equal chords are equidistant from the centre.

chapters.title: ANGLES IN A CIRCLE AND CYCLIC QUADRILATERAL

ANGLES IN A CIRCLE: Relationship between length of an arc and its degree measure
topics.description: Defines the types of angles formed in a circle, such as the central angle at the center and the inscribed angle on the circumference, and establishes the relationship between an arc's length and its degree measure.

PROPERTIES OF ANGLES IN A CIRCLE: The angle subtended at the centre of a circle by an arc is double the angle subtended by it on any point on the remaining part of the circle
Angle in a semicircle is a right angle
Theorem: Angles in the same segment of a circle are equal
Converse: If a line segment joining two points subtends equal angles at two other points on the same side of the line containing the segment, the four points lie on a circle
topics.description: Covers the fundamental theorems and properties governing the relationships between angles subtended by arcs and chords in a circle.

CONCYCLIC POINTS AND CYCLIC QUADRILATERA: Theorem: Sum of the opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral is 180°
Converse: If a pair of opposite angles of a quadrilateral is supplementary, then the quadrilateral is cyclic
topics.description: Introduces the concept of points lying on the same circle and focuses on cyclic quadrilaterals, exploring the key theorem about their opposite angles.

chapters.title: SECANTS, TANGENTS AND THEIR PROPERTIES

topics.description: Defines and differentiates between a secant, which intersects a circle at two distinct points, and a tangent, which touches a circle at exactly one point.

topics: Tangent and Radius Through the Point of Contact
Tangents from a Point Outside the Circle
Intersecting Chords Inside and Outside a Circle
Intersecting Secant and Tangent of a Circle
Angles Made by a Tangent and a Chord

chapters.title: CONSTRUCTIONS
topics: DIVISION OF A LINE SEGMENT IN THE GIVEN RATIO INTERNALLY

topics.description: This section covers the construction of triangles based on different given conditions such as sides, angles, perimeter, and medians.
subtopics: Two sides and the included angle is given (SAS)
Two angles and the included side are given (ASA)
A right triangle, when its hypotenuse and a side are given (RHS)
Perimeter and two base angles are given
Sum of two sides, third side and one of the angles on the third side are given
Difference of two sides, the third side and one of the angles on the third side are given
Two sides and a median corresponding to one of these sides, are given

topics: TO CONSTRUCT A TRIANGLE SIMILAR TO A GIVEN TRIANGLE, AS PER GIVEN SCALE FACTOR

topics.Topic Name: CONSTRUCTION OF TANGENTS TO A CIRCLE
topics.description: Methods for drawing tangents to a circle from a point on the circle and from a point outside the circle.
subtopics: To draw a tangent to a given circle at a given point on it using the centre of the circle
To draw tangents to a circle from a given point outside it

chapters.title: CO-ORDINATE GEOMETRY
topics: PLOTTING OF A POINT WHOSE CO-ORDINATES ARE GIVEN

topics.description: A formula to find the co-ordinates of a point which divides the line segment joining two points in a given ratio internally. A special case of this is the Mid-Point Formula.

chapters.title: PERIMETERS AND AREAS OF PLANE FIGURES

topics.description: Consolidation of formulae for finding the perimeters and areas of basic plane figures like rectangles, squares, parallelograms, triangles, rhombuses, and trapeziums.
Introduction and application of Heron's formula for finding the area of a triangle when the lengths of all three sides are known.

topics: Areas of Rectangular Paths and some Rectilinear Figures
Areas of Circles and Circular Paths

topics.description: Derivation and application of formulae for the perimeter and area of a sector of a circle, distinguishing between minor and major sectors.

topics: Areas of Combinations of Figures Involving Circles

chapters.title: SURFACE AREAS AND VOLUMES OF SOLID FIGURES
topics: Meanings of Surface Area and Volume

topics.description: Discusses the properties of cuboids and cubes, including their faces, edges, and vertices, and provides formulas for calculating their surface area, diagonal length, and volume.
Defines a right circular cylinder and explains how to calculate its curved surface area, total surface area, and volume.
Defines a right circular cone and its properties like radius, height, and slant height. Provides formulas for its curved surface area, total surface area, and volume.

Sphere and Hemisphere: Surface Areas of sphere and hemisphere
topics.description: Defines a sphere and a hemisphere, and provides the formulas to calculate their respective surface areas and volumes.

chapters.title: INTRODUCTION TO TRIGONOMETRY
topics: Trigonometric Ratios of an Acute Angle of a Right Angled Triangle
Given Two Sides of a Right-Triangle, to Find Trigonometric Ratio
Given One Trigonometric Ratio, to Find the Others

topics.description: Establishes the fundamental relationships between trigonometric ratios (reciprocal and quotient relations) and introduces the core trigonometric identities, including the Pythagorean identities.

chapters.title: TRIGONOMETRIC RATIOS OF SOME SPECIAL ANGLES
topics: Expected Background Knowledge: Pythagoras Theorem and Basic Trigonometric Ratios

topics.description: This section covers the geometric derivation and values of trigonometric ratios for the special angles 30°, 45°, and 60°. It also provides the values for 0° and 90° and summarizes them in a table.
Introduces concepts like angle of elevation and angle of depression to solve simple problems related to heights and distances in daily life.
Introduces statistics as a branch of mathematics, its meaning in singular and plural forms, and its application in daily life.
Explains the methods of collecting data, distinguishing between data collected firsthand (primary) and data from existing sources (secondary).

Presentation of Data: Class, Class Mark, Class Limits, and Class Size
topics.description: Covers methods to organize and condense data, including arranging it in order and creating frequency tables for both ungrouped and grouped data, along with related terminology.
Details the graphical methods for representing data, including the construction and interpretation of bar charts for discrete data, and histograms and frequency polygons for continuous grouped data.

Study of the arithmetical average, also known as the mean, which is a representative or central value of the data. Includes methods for raw, ungrouped, and grouped data using direct, short-cut (assumed mean), and step-deviation methods.
Study of the median, a measure of central tendency that gives the value of the middle-most observation when data is arranged in ascending or descending order.
Study of the mode, which is the observation that occurs most frequently in a dataset.

Introduces the concept of a random experiment as one with multiple possible outcomes that cannot be predicted in advance. It provides examples such as tossing a coin, throwing a die, and drawing a card from a deck.

PROBABILITY OF AN EVENT: Definition of an event and an elementary event
Formula for probability: P(E) = (Number of outcomes favourable to E) / (Number of all possible outcomes of the experiment)
topics.description: Defines the probability of an event for experiments with equally likely outcomes. It distinguishes between outcomes and events and provides the fundamental formula for calculating probability.

MORE ABOUT PROBABILITY: Sum of probabilities of all elementary events is 1
Complementary Events and the formula P(E) + P(Ē) = 1
topics.description: Discusses key properties of probability, including the probabilities of sure and impossible events, the numerical range of any probability, and the concept of complementary events.
This section covers the two primary methods for representing a set: the Roster method, which lists all elements, and the Set-builder form, which defines elements by a common property.
This topic introduces different types of sets based on their properties and number of elements, such as finite, infinite, empty, singleton, equal, equivalent, and disjoint sets.

Sub-Set: Number of Subsets of a Set
Intervals as Subsets of Real Numbers
topics.description: Explains the concept of a subset, how to calculate the number of subsets for a given set, and how intervals of real numbers can be represented as subsets.
Defines the complement of a set with respect to a universal set and discusses its properties, including Complement Laws and De Morgan's Laws.

CARTESIAN PRODUCT OF TWO SETS: Number of elements in the Cartesian product of two finite sets
Cartesian product of the set of real numbers R with itself
topics.description: Discusses the cartesian product of sets, its properties, and how to determine the number of elements in the product of finite sets.

RELATIONS: Domain and Range of a Relation
topics.description: Defines a relation as a subset of the Cartesian product and explains its domain, range, and co-domain.

DEFINITION OF A FUNCTION: Real valued function of a real variable
Some More Examples on Domain and Range
topics.description: Introduces a function as a special type of relation where every element in the domain has a unique image, and covers real valued functions.
Details various types of special functions and their properties.

Sum, difference, product and quotient of: Subtraction of a real function from another
topics.description: Explains how to perform arithmetic operations on real functions.

chapters.title: TRIGONOMETRIC FUNCTIONS-I

Circular Measure of Angle: Relation Between Length of an Arc and Radius of the Circle
topics.description: Introduces the concept of angles in terms of radians, its relation to degrees, and the formula relating arc length, radius, and the subtended angle.

Trigonometric Functions: Defining Trigonometric Functions on a Unit Circle
topics.description: Defines trigonometric functions (sin, cos, tan, etc.) as coordinates on a unit circle and explores the fundamental identities and relationships between them, such as sin²θ + cos²θ = 1.

topics: Trigonometric Functions of Some Specific Real Numbers

Graphs of Trigonometric Functions: Variations and Graph of sin θ
Variations and Graph of cos θ
Variations and Graph of tan θ
Variations and Graph of cot θ
Variations and Graph of sec θ
Variations and Graph of cosec θ
topics.description: Details the graphical representation of the six trigonometric functions, analyzing their variations, maximum and minimum values, and continuity over the interval 0 to 2π.
Explains the concept of periodicity and determines the period for each of the six trigonometric functions.

chapters.title: TRIGONOMETRIC FUNCTIONS-II

ADDITION AND MULTIPLICATION OF TRIGONOME: Addition and Subtraction Formulae for sin(A±B), cos(A±B), tan(A±B), and cot(A±B)
topics.description: This section establishes and applies the formulae for trigonometric functions of the sum or difference of two angles.

TRANSFORMATION OF PRODUCTS INTO SUMS AND: Transformation of Products into Sums or Differences
Transformation of Sums or Differences into Products
Further Applications of Addition and Subtraction Formulae
topics.description: Covers the conversion between products of trigonometric functions and sums/differences, also known as product-to-sum and sum-to-product formulae.

TRIGONOMETRIC FUNCTIONS OF MULTIPLES OF: Trigonometric Functions of 3A in Terms of A
topics.description: Derives and applies formulae for double angles (e.g., sin 2A, cos 2A) and triple angles (e.g., sin 3A, cos 3A).

topics: TRIGONOMETRIC FUNCTIONS OF SUBMULTIPLES OF ANGLES

TRIGONOMETRIC EQUATIONS: General solution of the equation sin θ = sin α
General solution of the equation cos θ = cos α
General solution of the equation tan θ = tan α
topics.description: Introduces methods for finding the general solutions to basic trigonometric equations.

chapters.title: RELATIONS BETWEEN SIDES AND ANGLES OF A TRIANGLE

topics.description: Introduction to establishing results that give the relationship between sides and angles of a triangle. The objectives are to derive and apply the sine, cosine, and projection formulas. Prerequisite knowledge includes trigonometric functions and various related formulae.
Proves that in any triangle, the lengths of the sides are proportional to the sines of the angles opposite to the sides, i.e., a/sin A = b/sin B = c/sin C.
Proves the relationship between the sides and angles of a triangle, expressed as cos A = (b² + c² - a²) / 2bc, cos B = (c² + a² - b²) / 2ac, and cos C = (a² + b² - c²) / 2ab.
Proves that in any triangle, a side can be expressed in terms of the other sides and their adjacent angles: a = b cos C + c cos B, b = c cos A + a cos C, and c = a cos B + b cos A.

chapters.title: SEQUENCES AND SERIES

6.2 ARITHMETIC PROGRESSION: 6.2.1 GENERAL TERM OF AN A. P.
topics.description: Introduction to Arithmetic Progression (A.P.), its definition, common difference, and the formula to find the general (nth) term.

topics: 6.3 TO FIND THE SUM OF FIRST n TERMS IN AN A. P.

topics.description: Introduction to Geometric Progression (G.P.), its common ratio, the formula for the general term, and some of its key properties.

topics: 6.6 SUM OF n TERMS OF A G. P.

6.7 INFINITE GEOMETRIC PROGRESSION: 6.7.1 SUM OF INFINITE TERMS OF A G. P.
topics.description: Discusses the concept of finding the sum of an infinite number of terms in a G.P. and the conditions under which it is possible.

6.8 GEOMETRIC MEAN (G. M.): 6.8.1 RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN A. M. AND G.M.
topics.description: Defines the Geometric Mean (G.M.) between numbers and establishes the mathematical relationship between the Arithmetic Mean (A.M.) and Geometric Mean.
Outlines the learning goals for the chapter, including defining a series, calculating terms, and evaluating sums of specific sequences like Σn, Σn², and Σn³.

7.2 SUM OF THE POWERS OF THE FIRST n NAT: Sum of the first n natural numbers (Σn)
Sum of the squares of the first n natural numbers (Σn²)
Sum of the cubes of the first n natural numbers (Σn³)
topics.description: Derivation and application of formulas for the sum of the first n natural numbers, the sum of the squares of the first n natural numbers, and the sum of the cubes of the first n natural numbers.

chapters.title: COMPLEX NUMBERS

topics.description: Defining the conjugate of a complex number (z = a - bi) and exploring its fundamental properties.

topics: GEOMETRICAL REPRESENTATION OF A COMPLEX NUMBER

topics.description: Defining the modulus or absolute value of a complex number and its key properties.

ADDITION OF COMPLEX NUMBERS: Geometrical Represention of Addition of Two Complex Numbers
topics.description: Rules for adding and subtracting complex numbers, and visualizing addition on the Argand Plane.
Examining the algebraic properties that govern the addition of complex numbers.

PROPERTIES OF MULTIPLICATION OF TWO COMP: Distributive Property of Multiplication over Addition
topics.description: Examining the algebraic properties related to the multiplication of complex numbers.

topics: SQUARE ROOT OF A COMPLEX NUMBER

chapters.title: QUADRATIC EQUATIONS AND LINEAR INEQUALITIES

SOLVING QUADRATIC EQUATION BY QUADRATIC: Nature of Roots based on Discriminant (D > 0, D = 0, D < 0)
topics.description: Explains how to solve quadratic equations using the quadratic formula and how to determine the nature of the roots (real, unequal, equal, or complex) by analyzing the discriminant.

topics: RELATION BETWEEN ROOTS AND COEFFICIENTS OF A QUADRATIC EQUATION
SOLUTION OF A QUADRATIC EQUATION WHEN D < 0
SOLUTIONS OF LINEAR INEQUALITIES IN ONE/TWO VARIABLES
GRAPHICAL REPRESENTATION OF LINEAR INEQUALITIES IN ONE OR TWO VARIABLES
GRAPHICAL SOLUTION OF A SYSTEM OF LINEAR INEQUATIONS IN TWO VARIABLES

topics.description: An introduction to different forms of reasoning, contrasting deductive logic (applying general principles to specific cases) with inductive logic (formulating general rules from specific observations), and presenting mathematical induction as a precise form of inductive reasoning.
Defines a mathematical statement as an assertion that is either true or false. It introduces the P(n) notation to represent statements involving a general natural number n.

10.2 The Principle of Mathematical Induc: The Inductive Step (Assuming P(k) is true and proving P(k+1) is true)
topics.description: Formally states the principle of mathematical induction as a method to prove that a statement P(n) is true for every natural number n. This involves a two-step process: verifying the base case and proving the inductive step.

Applications of Mathematical Induction: Proving Statements for n ≥ a, where a > 1
topics.description: Illustrates through various examples how to apply the principle of mathematical induction to prove different types of mathematical propositions, such as series summations, divisibility rules, and inequalities.

chapters.title: PERMUTATIONS AND COMBINATIONS

Permutations: Permutation of r objects out of n objects
topics.description: An arrangement of objects in a particular order. The section covers the concept of factorials (n!) and the formula for the number of permutations of r objects out of n objects (nPr).
A selection of objects where the order does not matter. This section introduces the formula for the number of combinations of r objects out of n objects (nCr) and discusses its properties.

topics: Problems Involving Both Permutations and Combinations

chapters.title: BINOMIAL THEOREM

The Binomial Theorem for a Natural Expon: Statement of the theorem for a positive integral index
Proof by the principle of mathematical induction
Applications in expanding expressions and evaluating numbers
topics.description: Introduction to the binomial theorem for expanding expressions of the form (x+y)^n, where n is a natural number, including its statement and proof.

topics: General Term in a Binomial Expansion

Middle Term(s) in a Binomial Expansion: Case 1: When the exponent n is even
Case 2: When the exponent n is odd
topics.description: Methods for finding the middle term or terms in a binomial expansion, which differs based on whether the exponent is an even or odd integer.

chapters.title: CARTESIAN SYSTEM OF RECTANGULAR CO-ORDINATES

topics.description: Introduction to the Cartesian coordinate system, including the origin, x-axis, y-axis, and the four quadrants. Defines how to locate a point using an ordered pair (abscissa, ordinate).
Formulas to find the coordinates of a point dividing a line segment in a given ratio, both internally and externally. Includes the midpoint formula.

topics: Condition for Collinearity of Three Points
Inclination and Slope of a Line
Slope of a Line Joining Two Distinct Points

topics.description: Discusses the relationship between the slopes of parallel lines (m1 = m2) and perpendicular lines (m1 * m2 = -1).

topics: Intercepts Made by a Line on Axes

chapters.title: STRAIGHT LINES
topics: Straight Line Parallel to an Axis

topics.description: This section covers the derivation of the equation of a straight line in various standard forms based on given conditions like slope, intercepts, and points.

General Equation of First Degree: Conversion of General Equation of a Line into Various Forms
topics.description: Discusses the general form of a linear equation (Ax + By + C = 0) and methods to convert it into other standard forms of a straight line.

topics: Distance of a Given Point from a Given Line

Equation of Parallel (or Perpendicular): Equation of a Straight Line Parallel to the Given Line
Straight Line Perpendicular to the Given Line
topics.description: Methods to find the equation of a line that is either parallel or perpendicular to another given line.

topics: Equation of Family of Lines Passing Through the Point of Intersection of Two Lines

topics.description: Defines a circle as the locus of a point moving in a plane at a constant distance (radius) from a fixed point (centre).

15.2 EQUATION OF A CIRCLE: 15.2.1 WHEN COORDINATES OF THE CENTRE AND RADIUS ARE GIVEN
topics.description: Derives the standard form of the equation of a circle, (x-h)² + (y-k)² = a², where (h,k) is the centre and 'a' is the radius. It also explores special cases, such as when the circle passes through the origin or touches the axes.

15.3 GENERAL EQUATION OF THE CIRCLE IN S: 15.3.1 CONDITONS UNDER WHICH THE GENERAL EQUATION OF SECOND DEGREE IN TWO VARIABLES REPRESENTS A CIRCLE
topics.description: Introduces the general form of a circle's equation, x² + y² + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0. It explains how to determine the centre (-g, -f) and radius (sqrt(g² + f² - c)) from this equation and lists the conditions for a general second-degree equation to represent a circle.

Introduces the mathematical definition of a conic section as the locus of a point based on its distance from a fixed point (focus) and a fixed line (directrix), governed by a constant ratio called eccentricity (e).
Defines an ellipse as a conic where e < 1. Covers the derivation of its standard equation and defines key properties such as major axis, minor axis, latus rectum, and foci.
Defines a parabola as a conic where e = 1. Derives the standard equation y² = 4ax and discusses other forms (y² = -4ax, x² = 4ay, x² = -4ay) along with their corresponding properties.

HYPERBOLA: Equation of Hyperbola in Standard from
topics.description: Defines a hyperbola as a conic where e > 1. Details the derivation of its standard equation and related concepts like transverse axis, conjugate axis, foci, and the special case of a rectangular hyperbola.

chapters.title: MEASURES OF DISPERSION

topics.description: Defines and introduces various statistical measures to quantify the spread or scatteredness of a dataset, including range, mean deviation, variance, and standard deviation.

topics: 17.3 MEAN DEVIATION FROM MEAN OF RAW AND GROUPED DATA

topics.description: Provides methods to calculate the median for both discrete and continuous frequency distributions of grouped data.

17.5 MEAN DEVIATION FROM MEDIAN: 17.5.1 MEAN DEVIATION OF GROUPED DATA FROM MEDIAN
topics.description: Explains the procedure to calculate the mean deviation of observations from the median for both raw and grouped data.

topics: 17.6 VARIANCE AND STANDARD DEVIATION OF RAW DATA
17.7 STANDARD DEVIATION AND VARIANCE OF RAW DATA AN ALTERNATE METHOD
17.8 STANDARD DEVIATION AND VARIANCE OF GROUPED DATA: METHOD - I
17.9 STANDARD DEVIATION AND VARIANCE OF GROUPED DATA: METHOD - II
17.10 STANDARD DEVIATION AND VARIANCE: STEP DEVIATION METHOD

17.11 PROPERTIES OF VARIANCE AND STANDAR: Property I: Independence of change of origin
Property II: Dependence on the change of scale
Property III: Standard deviation is the least possible root mean square deviation
Property IV: Combining standard deviations of two sets
topics.description: Discusses the key mathematical properties of variance and standard deviation, such as their behavior with changes in origin and scale, and how to combine them for multiple datasets.

topics: 17.12 ANALYSIS OF FREQUENCY DISTRIBUTIONS WITH EQUAL MEANS

topics.description: Defines an experiment as an activity that yields a result or an outcome. A random experiment is one that can be repeated under identical conditions, but whose outcome is not predictable beforehand due to the role of chance.

Introduces the concept of a sample space (S) as the set of all possible outcomes of an experiment. The elements of the sample space are called sample points. Examples include tossing coins, rolling dice, and combinations of these activities, often visualized with tree diagrams.

Defines an event as a subset of the sample space. It further classifies events based on their properties: equally likely (no outcome is preferred), mutually exclusive (cannot happen simultaneously), exhaustive (their union covers the entire sample space), and independent/dependent (the occurrence of one does or does not affect the other).

chapters.title: PROBABILITY

topics.description: Defines the classical probability of an event as the ratio of the number of favorable outcomes to the total number of equally likely outcomes.
Utilizes permutations and combinations to determine the number of possible and favorable outcomes, especially in scenarios with large sample spaces.
Describes the relationships between different events using set theory concepts like complement, union (or), and intersection (and).

topics: 19.5 ADDITIVE LAW OF PROBABILITY FOR MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE EVENTS
19.6 MULTIPLICATION LAW OF PROBABILITY FOR INDEPENDENT EVENTS
19.8 THEOREMS ON MULTIPLICATION LAW OF PROBABILITY AND CONDITIONAL PROBABILITY

topics.description: Introduces the concept of finding the probability of an event that occurred prior to another given event. It covers the theorem of total probability and the formal statement and application of Baye's theorem.

19.12 PROBABILITY DISTRIBUTION OF RANDOM: 19.12.3 Probability Distribution of a Random Variable
topics.description: Defines a random variable and explains how to create its probability distribution, which lists all possible values of the variable and their corresponding probabilities.
Defines and provides formulas for calculating the mean (expected value) and variance of a discrete random variable.
Describes the probability distribution for the number of successes in a fixed number of independent Bernoulli trials.
Introduction to the concept of matrices, how they are represented, and their order.
Classification of matrices based on their structure and elements.
Discusses the transpose of a matrix and special types like symmetric and skew-symmetric matrices.
Covers the process of adding matrices and the properties associated with matrix addition.

Elementary Transformations or Elementary: Inverse of a Matrix by Elementary Operations
topics.description: Explains the elementary row and column operations and their application in finding the inverse of a matrix.

chapters.title: DETERMINANTS
topics: 21.2 EXPANSION OF A DETERMINANT OF ORDER 2
21.5 EXPANSION OF A DETERMINANT OF ORDER 3

topics.description: Introduces the concepts of minors and cofactors of elements within a determinant. The minor is the value of the determinant obtained by deleting the row and column of an element, while the cofactor is the signed minor.

21.7 PROPERTIES OF DETERMINANTS: Property 1: The value of a determinant remains unchanged if its rows and columns are interchanged.
Property 2: If two rows (or columns) of a determinant are interchanged, then the value of the determinant changes in sign only.
Property 3: If any two rows (or columns) of a determinant are identical then the value of the determinant is zero.
Property 4: If each element of a row (or column) of a determinant is multiplied by the same constant, then the value of the determinant is multiplied by that constant.
Property 5: If each element of a row (or of a column) is expressed as the sum of two or more terms, then the determinant can be expressed as the sum of two or more determinants.
Property 6: The value of a determinant does not change if a multiple of one row (or column) is added to another row (or column).
topics.description: Details the fundamental properties that simplify the evaluation of determinants.

topics: 21.8 EVALUATION OF A DETERMINANT USING PROPERTIES

21.11 Application of Determinants: 21.11.2 Condition of collinearity of three points
21.11.2 Equation of a line passing through the given two points
topics.description: Covers the practical applications of determinants, including calculating the area of a triangle, determining if three points lie on the same line, and finding the equation of a line.

chapters.title: INVERSE OF A MATRIX AND ITS APPLICATIONS

topics.description: Introduction to the determinant associated with a square matrix and the classification of matrices as singular (|A| = 0) or non-singular (|A| ≠ 0).

topics: MINORS AND COFACTORS OF THE ELEMENTS OF SQUARE MATRIX

SOLUTION OF A SYSTEM OF LINEAR EQUATIONS: CRITERION FOR CONSISTENCY OF A SYSTEM OF EQUATIONS
topics.description: Application of matrices to solve systems of linear equations using the matrix inversion method (AX = B gives X = A⁻¹B). It also covers the criteria to determine if a system of equations is consistent (has a solution) or inconsistent.
Defines a relation as a subset of the Cartesian product of sets and introduces key types of relations: reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.
Categorizes functions based on their mapping properties, such as one-to-one, many-to-one, onto, and bijective functions.
Introduces the concept of a binary operation on a set and its fundamental properties like commutativity, associativity, identity, and inverse elements.

chapters.title: INVERSE TRIGONOMETRIC FUNCTIONS

OBJECTIVES: state the condition for the inverse of trigonometric functions to exist
define the principal value of inverse trigonometric functions
find domain and range of inverse trigonometric functions
state the properties of inverse trigonometric functions
simplify expressions involving inverse trigonometric functions

topics: 24.1 IS INVERSE OF EVERY FUNCTION POSSIBLE ?

topics.description: Discussion on restricting the domain of trigonometric functions to make them invertible, leading to the concept of the principal value branch for each inverse trigonometric function.

24.3 PROPERTIES OF INVERSE TRIGONOMETRIC: Property 2: Reciprocal relationships, e.g., cosec⁻¹x = sin⁻¹(1/x)
Property 3: Properties of negative arguments, e.g., sin⁻¹(-x) = -sin⁻¹x and cos⁻¹(-x) = π - cos⁻¹x
Property 4: Complementary function relationships, e.g., sin⁻¹x + cos⁻¹x = π/2
Property 5: Sum and difference formulas for tan⁻¹x
Property 6: Formulas for 2tan⁻¹x in terms of sin⁻¹, cos⁻¹, and tan⁻¹
Property 7: Interconversion between inverse trigonometric functions, e.g., expressing sin⁻¹x in terms of other inverse functions

chapters.title: LIMIT AND CONTINUITY

25.1 LIMIT OF A FUNCTION: Methods for calculating limits (Substitution, Factorization, Rationalization)
topics.description: Introduction to the concept of the limiting value of a function as the independent variable approaches a specific point.

topics: 25.3 LIMIT OF FUNCTION y = f(x) AT x = a

25.4 BASIC THEOREMS ON LIMITS: Limit of a constant multiple of a function
Limit of a sum or difference of functions
Limit of a product of functions
Limit of a quotient of functions
topics.description: Theorems governing the calculation of limits for functions combined through basic arithmetic operations.

25.5 FINDING LIMITS OF SOME OF THE IMPOR: Limits of trigonometric functions (sin x, cos x, sin x/x)
topics.description: Derivation and application of standard limits for algebraic, trigonometric, exponential, and logarithmic functions.

topics: 25.6 CONTINUITY OF A FUNCTION AT A POINT
25.7 DISCONTINUITY OF A FUNCTION AT A POINT

topics.description: Examining the continuity of standard types of functions and their properties.

chapters.title: DIFFERENTIATION
topics: 26.5 DERIVATIVE OF A FUNCTION FROM FIRST PRINCIPLE

ALGEBRA OF DERIVATIVES: 26.7 DERIVATIVES OF SUM AND DIFFERENCE OF FUNCTIONS
topics.description: This section introduces the rules for finding derivatives of functions that are combinations (sum, difference, product, quotient) of other functions, as well as the rule for composite functions (function of a function).

Outlines the learning goals for the chapter, including finding derivatives of trigonometric and inverse trigonometric functions from first principles, applying differentiation rules, and finding second-order derivatives.

27.1 DERIVATIVE OF TRIGONOMETRIC FUNCTIO: Derivative of cot x and cosec x (mentioned)
topics.description: This section explains how to find the derivatives of basic trigonometric functions using the fundamental definition of a derivative (the first principle).
This section covers finding the derivatives of more complex trigonometric functions by applying standard differentiation rules such as the product rule, quotient rule, and chain rule.
This section demonstrates how to derive the formulas for the derivatives of standard inverse trigonometric functions starting from the first principle.
Focuses on applying the established derivative formulas for inverse trigonometric functions to solve problems, often in combination with the chain rule.
Explains the concept of finding the second derivative of a function, which is the derivative of the first derivative, with examples involving trigonometric and inverse trigonometric functions.

chapters.title: DIFFERENTIATION OF EXPONENTIAL AND LOGARITHMIC FUNCTIONS

topics.description: This section covers the derivation from first principles and the working rule for finding the derivatives of exponential functions, particularly for e^x and e^(ax+b).

Derivative of Logarithmic Functions: Using logarithm properties to simplify differentiation
topics.description: This section explains how to find the derivative of logarithmic functions. It also demonstrates the technique of taking logarithms on both sides to simplify the differentiation of complex products and quotients.

Derivative of Logarithmic Function (Cont: Derivative of functions in the form [f(x)]^g(x)
topics.description: Focuses on the technique of logarithmic differentiation, which is used when the exponent in a function is a variable. This method is applied to find the derivative of functions like a^x and x^x.

Derivative of Parametric Functions: First Order Derivative of Parametric Functions
Second Order Derivative of Parametric Functions
topics.description: Introduces parametric functions and the chain rule method to find their first and second order derivatives with respect to a variable.

chapters.title: APPLICATIONS OF DERIVATIVES

topics.description: Using derivatives to find the rate at which one quantity changes with respect to another, including concepts like marginal revenue and marginal cost.

APPROXIMATIONS: Using differentials to find approximate values
topics.description: Understanding and calculating small changes and errors in dependent variables corresponding to small changes in independent variables.

topics: EQUATIONS OF TANGENT AND NORMAL TO A CURVE

topics.description: An extension of Rolle's Theorem stating that for a continuous and differentiable function, there exists a point where the tangent is parallel to the secant line through the endpoints.
Defining and identifying intervals where a function is increasing or decreasing based on the sign of its derivative.

topics: RELATION BETWEEN THE SIGN OF THE DERIVATIVE AND MONOTONICITY OF FUNCTION

MAXIMUM AND MINIMUM VALUES OF A FUNCTION: Method of Finding Maxima or Minima (First Derivative Test)
topics.description: Finding local maxima and minima (extreme values) of a function by analyzing the sign change of the first derivative around stationary points.

topics: USE OF SECOND DERIVATIVE FOR DETERMINATION OF MAXIMUM AND MINIMUM VALUES OF A FUNCTION
APPLICATIONS OF MAXIMA AND MINIMA TO PRACTICAL PROBLEMS

TECHNIQUES OF INTEGRATION: Integration of Function of The Type f'(x) / f(x)
Integration of tan x, cot x, sec x, and cosec x
Integration of standard algebraic and trigonometric forms
topics.description: This section covers various methods to find integrals, including the substitution method, integration of specific trigonometric functions, and evaluation of integrals for standard algebraic expressions like 1/(x² ± a²), 1/√(a² ± x²), etc.

chapters.title: DEFINITE INTEGRALS

Definite Integral as a Limit of Sum: Average Value of a Function in an Interval
topics.description: Defining and interpreting the definite integral geometrically as the limit of a sum, which involves dividing an area into an infinite number of subintervals.

topics: Evaluation of Definite Integral by Substitution

Applications of Integration: Area Bounded by the Curve, x-axis and the Ordinates
Area Bounded by the Curve x = f(y) between y-axis and Lines
topics.description: Using definite integrals to calculate the area of regions bounded by curves, axes, and lines.

Fundamentals of Differential Equations: Order and Degree of a differential equation
topics.description: An introduction to differential equations, covering their definition, the concepts of order and degree, and the distinction between linear and non-linear forms.

Techniques of Solving a Differential Equ: Linear Differential Equation of the type dy/dx + P(x)y = Q(x)
topics.description: Covers various methods for finding the solution to first-order differential equations, including the method of separation of variables, solving homogeneous equations, and solving linear equations using an integrating factor.

Coordinate System and Coordinates of a P: Coordinates of a point in XY-plane, YZ-plane, and ZX-plane
Coordinates of a point on X-axis, Y-axis, and Z-axis
topics.description: Introduction to the three-dimensional Cartesian coordinate system, defining the origin, axes, and planes. It explains how to determine the coordinates of a point in space and identifies the eight octants.

Distance Between Two Points: Distance of a Point from the Origin
topics.description: Derivation and application of the formula to calculate the distance between two points in three-dimensional space, and a special case for calculating the distance of a point from the origin.

Derivation of the section formula to find the coordinates of a point that divides a line segment joining two points in a given ratio, both internally and externally, including the formula for the mid-point.

chapters.title: VECTORS
topics: 34.2 VECTOR AS A DIRECTED LINE SEGMENT

topics.description: Classification of vectors based on their magnitude, direction, and initial points.

34.4 ADDITION OF VECTORS: 34.4.1 Triangle Law of Addition of Vectors
34.4.2 Addition of more than two Vectors
34.4.3 Parallelogram Law of Addition of Vectors
34.4.5 The Difference of Two Given Vectors
topics.description: Explains the rules and methods for adding and subtracting vectors, including the triangle and parallelogram laws.

topics: 34.6 MULTIPLICATION OF A VECTOR BY A SCALAR
34.8 RESOLUTION OF A VECTOR ALONG TWO PERPENDICULAR AXES
34.9 RESOLUTION OF A VECTOR IN THREE DIMENSIONS ALONG THREE MUTUALLY PERPENDICULAR AXES

chapters.title: LINEAR PROGRAMMING

topics.description: Defines the fundamental components of a linear programming problem, including the objective function to be optimized, the constraints or limitations on resources, and the non-negative restrictions on decision variables.

topics: 37.3 FORMULATION OF A LINEAR PROGRAMMING PROBLEM
37.4 GEOMETRIC APPORACH OF LINEAR PROGRAMMING PROBLEM

topics.description: Explains the graphical method for solving linear programming problems, introducing the concepts of a feasible solution that satisfies all constraints, and an optimal solution that optimizes the objective function.
Defines a mathematically acceptable statement as a sentence that is either true or false, but not both. Differentiates between valid (true) and invalid (false) statements.

Explains the two types of statements in mathematical reasoning: simple statements that cannot be broken down, and compound statements formed by combining two or more simple statements using connecting words like 'and' or 'or'.

Discusses techniques and rules to determine the validity (truth) of mathematical statements, including compound statements with 'and'/'or', 'if-then' implications, and 'if and only if' conditions. It covers validation through direct method, contrapositive method, and method of contradiction.
